data.title: UK's trade deficit widens in April
data.description:
The British economy shrank 0.3% M/M in April 2025, the first decline in six months, and the biggest since October 2024. It follows a 0.2% growth in March and compares with forecasts of a 0.1% decline.

Services output fell by 0.4%, following growth of 0.4% in March 2025, and was the largest contributor to the fall in GDP. 

The UK trade deficit widened sharply to £7.03 billion in April 2025 from £3.70 billion in March. This marked the largest trade gap since June 2022, driven by falling exports and rising imports. Exports dropped by 3.4%, while goods imports grew 1.2%.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB)

Currency: (GBP:USD)

data.title: Calavo Growers stock soars on $32 per share takeover bid
data.description:
Calavo Growers (NASDAQ:CVGW) has announced that it received an unsolicited, non-binding proposal to acquire all outstanding shares at $32.00 per share.

The identity of the party making the offer has not been disclosed.

The proposed deal structure includes a combination of stock from the acquiring party and cash. 

The company's board is currently reviewing the proposal with legal and financial advisors.

Lastly, the company said that it does not intend to comment or update further unless warranted.

The announcement has caused Calavo Growers' stock (NASDAQ:CVGW) to surge 12.6% after-hours on Wednesday.

data.title: CPKC announces C$1.4 billion debt offering
data.description:
• Canadian Pacific Kansas City (NYSE:CP)  announced that its wholly owned subsidiary, Canadian Pacific Railway, will issue C$500 million in 4.00% Notes due 2032, C$600 million in 4.40% Notes due 2036, and C$300 million in 4.80% Notes due 2055.
  • The notes, part of a new offering (the "Offering"), will be guaranteed by CPKC.
  • The transaction is expected to close on June 13, 2025.
  • The net proceeds from this offering will be used to refinance CPRC's outstanding indebtedness and for general corporate purposes.

data.title: China-backed militia secures control of new rare earth mines in Myanmar - report
data.description:
A Chinese-backed militia is protecting new rare earth mines in eastern Myanmar, as Beijing moves to secure control of the minerals it is wielding as a bargaining chip in its trade war with Washington, Reuters reported.

Beijing depends heavily on Myanmar for the raw, rare earth metals and oxides essential for products such as wind turbines, medical equipment, and electric vehicles.

According to Chinese customs data, nearly half of these imports came from the conflict-ridden country during the first four months of this year.

However, Beijing’s access to new supplies of key minerals such as dysprosium and terbium has been severely restricted after a major mining region in northern Myanmar was seized by an armed group fighting the country’s military junta—an ally of China.

According to the report, Chinese miners are now opening new deposits for extraction in the hillsides of Shan state in eastern Myanmar, and at least 100 people are working day and night shifts excavating hillsides and extracting minerals using chemicals.

Two residents of the area said they had witnessed trucks carrying material from the mines, between the towns of Mong Hsat and Mong Yun, toward the Chinese border some 200km away. Reuters identified some of the sites using imagery from commercial satellite providers Planet Labs (NYSE:PL) and Maxar Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR).

The mines operate under the protection of the United Wa State Army, according to four sources, two of whom were able to identify the uniforms of the militia members, the report said.

data.title: Oracle in charts: Cloud services grow 27% Y/Y, while license support rev remain tepid in FQ4
Trump may extend July 8 deadline but adds won't be a necessity: 'take it or leave it'
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said he is open to extending the July 8 deadline for finishing up trade talks with several countries before higher U.S. tariffs take effect; however, he does not believe it will be necessary.

“We’re rocking in terms of deals,” he told reporters at the Kennedy Center. “We’re dealing with quite a few countries and they all want to make a deal with us,” he showed confidence in progress over trade talks. Trump stressed that he didn't see an extension as being “a necessity.”

“At a certain point, we’re just going to send letters out. And I think you understand that, saying this is the deal, you can take it or leave it,” Trump added. "So at a certain point we'll do that. We're not quite ready."

These letters will present final offers, essentially giving countries the option to accept or reject the terms.

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent provided further clarification, stating that the administration could "roll the date forward" for countries negotiating in good faith, allowing more time for discussions if needed.

"It is highly likely that those countries - or trading blocs as is the case with the EU - who are negotiating in good faith, we will roll the date forward to continue the good-faith negotiations," Bessent told the House Ways and Means Committee. "If someone is not negotiating, then we will not."

So far, only one trade deal with Britain has been finalized, while negotiations with about 17 others are at different stages.

data.title: Asia markets under pressure as Trump rekindles tariff fears and rising Iran tensions
data.description:
Asia stock markets trade mostly lower on Thursday, after Trump said he intended to send letters to trading partners in the next week or two setting unilateral tariff rates, ahead of a July 9 deadline to reimpose higher duties on dozens of countries.

US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent also suggested that the Trump administration could extend the 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for countries that show “good faith” in trade talks.

The US government on Wednesday ordered the departure of embassy staff from Baghdad after Iran threatened to attack US bases if talks over its nuclear program fall through.

Gold rose to around $3,370 per ounce on Thursday, as escalating US-Iran tensions and fresh trade policy signals from President Donald Trump boosted safe-haven demand. 

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 0.61% to below 38,200 while the broader Topix Index lost 0.3% to 2,780 on Thursday, with Japanese shares snapping a four-day winning streak The Japanese yen appreciated to around 144 per dollar on Thursday, extending gains from the previous session.

In Japan, business sentiment continued to weaken in the second quarter, reflecting rising uncertainty over US trade policy and its impact on the country’s export-dependent economy.

China (SHCOMP) rose 0.07% to below 3,400 while the Shenzhen Component shed 0.15 to 10,237 on Thursday, and the offshore yuan 

Meanwhile, the U.S. and China reached a framework to ease trade tensions, which Trump called “done” pending final approval from both leaders. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said tariffs on Chinese goods would not rise above the current 55%. However, the key details remain unclear, and no official confirmation has been issued by Beijing. 

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 0.43% to 24,281 in early trade Thursday, retreating from a near three-month high and snapping gains from the previous session.

India (SENSEX) fell 0.18% 

Australia (AS51) fell 0.09% to around 8,598 on Thursday. The Australian dollar depreciated past $0.649 on Thursday, extending its decline from the previous session.

Consumer inflation expectations in Australia jumped to 5.0% in June 2025 from 4.1% the previous month, marking the highest level since July 2023. 

In the U.S., on Wednesday, all three major indexes ended lower as investors took a breather from the recent rally, despite encouraging inflation data and a tentative US-China trade agreement.

Investors now turn their attention to Thursday’s PPI release for further clues on the inflation outlook and monetary policy path.

U.S. stock futures saw a sharp decline on Thursday, triggered by President Trump’s announcement that he will set unilateral tariff rates within two weeks: Dow -0.29%; S&P 500 -0.23%; Nasdaq -0.27%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia:

Japan’s producer prices rise 3.2% Y/Y in May, the least in 8 months

China’s May CPI declines fourth consecutive month, PPI deflation eases to two-year low

China's trade surplus widens amid weaker exports and deeper import slump

Japan's Q1 GDP revised upward, showing no growth but avoids contraction

China to send Vice Premier He Lifeng to UK for U.S. trade talks

data.title: Oracle targets over $67B revenue and 70% cloud infrastructure growth in FY26 while expanding global data center footprint
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Oracle Corporation (ORCL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Safra Catz opened by stating, "As you can see, we had an excellent fourth quarter to finish out an amazing year with Q4 total revenue and EPS both exceeding my guidance." Catz highlighted a "tipping point in our cloud transition," noting double-digit revenue growth and raising revenue guidance for fiscal year '26 to over $67 billion, up 16% for the year. She emphasized, "Our remaining performance obligations now stand at $138 billion, up $8 billion from last quarter and up 41% from last year, and yet the best is still to come."
  • Catz projected further acceleration for Oracle's cloud applications and infrastructure, with a specific focus on AI: "With the addition of over 100 AI agents, along with strong bookings and higher renewal rates for our strategic SaaS products, I expect the cloud applications growth rate will accelerate this coming year."
  • The infrastructure business is positioned for significant expansion, as Catz stated, "OCI has seen exceptional demand for infrastructure services and those contracted noncancelable bookings in RPO give us confidence that OCI revenue will grow over 70% this current year."
  • On capital deployment, Catz commented, "I expect that FY '26 CapEx will be higher at over $25 billion as we work to meet demand from our backlog."
  • CTO Larry Ellison declared, "Oracle will be the #1 cloud database company. Oracle will be the #1 cloud applications company, and Oracle will be the #1 builder and operator of cloud infrastructure data centers." He described the unique AI capabilities of Oracle's database, stating, "The latest vector version of the Oracle Database, Oracle 23 AI, is an AI data platform, the only database that can make all of the customer's data immediately available to all of the popular AI large language models while maintaining complete data privacy for the customer."

Outlook

  • Catz raised Oracle's fiscal year 2026 revenue guidance to at least $67 billion, representing 16% growth in constant currency. She added, "I expect that total cloud revenue will grow over 40% in constant currency, up from 24% in FY '25. I expect that cloud infrastructure revenue will grow over 70%, up from 51% in FY '25."
  • For Q1, Catz guided, "Total revenues are expected to grow from 11% to 13% in constant currency... Total cloud revenue is expected to grow from 26% to 30%... Non-GAAP EPS is expected to grow between 4% to 6% and be between $1.44 and $1.48 in constant currency."
  • Catz stated, "RPO is likely to grow more than 100% in fiscal '26. And lastly, I expect we will exceed the revenue growth target we previously provided for FY '27. Beyond FY '27, I'm even more confident in our ability to meet and likely exceed our previously provided FY '29 targets."

Financial Results

  • Total revenues for the quarter were $15.9 billion, up 11% from last year, as stated by Catz. Non-GAAP EPS was $1.70, while GAAP EPS was $1.19. Operating income grew 7% for the quarter.
  • Total cloud revenue, SaaS plus IaaS, was $6.7 billion, up 27%. IaaS revenue was $3 billion, up 52%. OCI consumption revenue was up 62%, with infrastructure cloud services now at an annualized revenue of nearly $12 billion.
  • Cloud database services were up 31% and have annualized revenue of $2.6 billion. Autonomous Database consumption revenue was up 47%.
  • Remaining performance obligation at the end of Q4 reached $138 billion, up 41%. Operating cash flow for Q4 was $6.2 billion. CapEx for the quarter was $9.1 billion, with full-year CapEx totaling $21.2 billion.

Q&A

  • Mark L. Moerdler, Bernstein: Asked for clarity on AI business durability and profitability. Ellison responded, "We have most of the world's valuable data. The vast majority of it is in an Oracle database. And the latest version... is an AI-centric piece of technology, a vector database called Oracle 23 AI."
  • John Stephen DiFucci, Guggenheim: Sought details on Stargate's inclusion in IaaS growth and RPO. Catz replied, "Stargate is still in formation... We are the destination for everyone who wants AI workloads... and all of that together comes in our RPO."
  • Benjamin Alexander Reitzes, Melius Research: Queried about the jump in CapEx and its impact. Catz said, "Our CapEx is usually about equipment... We are putting out as much capacity as we possibly can as quickly as we can. I do believe that the $25 billion next year may turn out to be understated."
  • Sitikantha Panigrahi, Mizuho: Asked about cloud database migration and revenue drivers. Catz emphasized, "Database support is up, is solid. License is up, all the cloud metrics, autonomous, consumption, Oracle Cloud, all of multi-cloud is basically using up all capacity that gets put out there."
  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays: Focused on applications performance. Catz explained, "Our strategic SaaS products as they roll out in our customer sites and as they ramp up, they're very, very popular. They're obviously compelling because only if you're in the cloud, can you use the AI capabilities."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed strong interest in the durability and scalability of Oracle's AI and cloud businesses, with a generally positive and congratulatory tone but persistent requests for clarity around specific growth drivers and contract details.
  • Management demonstrated high confidence, with Catz stating, "We have so much in pipeline right now that... we are still waving off customers... so that we have enough supply to meet demand." Ellison repeatedly asserted Oracle's technological advantages and leadership in cloud and AI.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management presented a heightened sense of optimism and urgency, with analysts focusing on the magnitude of growth and management offering more aggressive forward-looking statements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a significant increase in revenue guidance for FY26 to at least $67 billion, up from the prior $66 billion target. Cloud infrastructure growth expectations were also raised from "faster than 50%" to "over 70%."
  • Remaining performance obligations rose from $130 billion to $138 billion, with management now expecting over 100% RPO growth in FY26.
  • CapEx guidance increased from $16 billion to more than $25 billion to meet surging demand, compared to earlier conservative projections.
  • Management tone shifted from confidence to assertive optimism, and analysts' questions shifted toward understanding the scale, profitability, and durability of Oracle's AI and cloud businesses.
  • Strategic focus broadened to emphasize AI agent integration across applications, multi-cloud expansion, and accelerated infrastructure rollout.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged supply constraints: "I am still in a position where our supply is not meeting our demand... This is a situation that we have not seen in our history."
  • CapEx intensity was noted, with Catz stating, "I do believe that the $25 billion next year may turn out to be understated."
  • The ability to scale data centers and fulfill noncancelable contracts remains a top priority, as does managing the complexity of global partnerships and rapid customer migration.

Final Takeaway

Oracle's management highlights a year of robust growth and record-breaking performance, raising guidance for both revenue and cloud infrastructure expansion in fiscal 2026. With a $138 billion backlog, aggressive investments in data centers, and a growing portfolio of AI-enabled solutions, the company positions itself as a leading enabler of enterprise cloud and artificial intelligence, while continuing to manage unprecedented demand and capital investments to support future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BeOne gets FDA nod for tablet version of zanubrutinib
data.description:
BeOne Medicines (NASDAQ:ONC) said on Wednesday that it has received approval from the U.S. FDA for a new tablet version of its medication, zanubrutinib, a small molecule that inhibits Bruton’s tyrosine kinase.

This approval covers all five current indications for the drug.

The recommended dosage for zanubrutinib, also known as Brukinsa, is still 320 mg per day.

However, the newly approved Brukinsa tablets come in 160 mg doses, allowing patients to take just two tablets daily instead of the previous four 80 mg capsules, the company said.

The European Medicines Agency is currently reviewing a type II variation marketing authorization application for the new tablet formulation of Brukinsa in all currently approved indications, with approval expected later this year.
